1: Bezalel made the ark (aron) of acacia wood. The Spirit-filled craftsman from the tribe of Judah makes the holiest object. Christ — from the tribe of Judah (Hebrews 7:14; Revelation 5:5) — is the one who both builds and embodies the new dwelling of God: he is the temple (John 2:21), and he is the mercy seat atop the ark (Romans 3:25). The ark's craftsman and the ark's fulfillment come from the same royal tribe.
2: The ark overlaid inside and out with pure gold. Acacia overlaid with pure gold on every surface, within and without. The thorough penetration of divinity through the humanity of the holiest object: in Christ “all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form” (Colossians 2:9) — not only outward appearance but the inner substance of his humanity is penetrated with the divine life (John 1:14: “full of grace and truth”).
3: Rings of gold on the four corners of the ark, two rings on each side. The four rings for carrying poles: the ark moves through the wilderness with Israel, never fixed in one place. Christ accompanies his people wherever they go: “And surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age” (Matthew 28:20). The mobile ark is the type of the abiding Christ-presence that is not geographically confined.
4: Poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold to carry the ark. The carrying poles: Christ is borne through history by those who carry the word of the gospel. “How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news” (Romans 10:15, citing Isaiah 52:7). The ark's carriers are the type of those who bear Christ's presence into every place.
5: The poles inserted into the rings on the sides to carry the ark. The carrying poles set in their rings: the structure for transportation in place. The church in mission is the movement of the ark through the world — the presence of Christ going to every nation, tribe, and tongue (Matthew 28:18-20; Revelation 5:9).
6: The mercy seat (kapporet) of pure gold. Unlike the ark beneath it (acacia overlaid with gold), the mercy seat is pure gold throughout. It is YHWH's throne-footstool and the meeting point of God and Israel, sprinkled with blood on Yom Kippur. Paul calls Christ the hilasterion (Romans 3:25) — the LXX word for the mercy seat. Christ is both the blood-sprinkling high priest and the mercy seat on which the blood is placed: both offerer and offering, both priest and propitiation.
7: Two cherubim of hammered gold on the two ends of the mercy seat. The cherubim — guardians of divine holiness (Genesis 3:24) — are positioned looking down at the sprinkled blood. Where cherubim would bar the way (Genesis 3:24: “to guard the way to the tree of life”), the blood-covered mercy seat opens it. John 20:12: two angels at the empty tomb — one at the head, one at the feet — are the resurrection fulfillment of the cherubim on the kapporet. The atonement is complete; the guardians witness it.
8: The cherubim are one piece with the mercy seat at each end. The place of atonement and its guardians are unified: the very holiness that bars the way is reconciled with the mercy that opens it in the one object. At the empty tomb (John 20:12), the angels witness the completed atonement — the guardians of holiness are present because the blood has fully satisfied the demand. 1 Peter 1:12: “angels long to look into these things.”
9: The cherubim spread their wings upward, overshadowing the mercy seat, faces toward each other looking down toward it. The cherubim's gaze directed to the blood below them: the angels' fixed attention on the atonement. “There I will meet with you, and from above the mercy seat... I will speak with you” (Exodus 25:22). The meeting point of God and humanity is where blood and mercy intersect — Christ is that meeting point (1 Timothy 2:5).
10: The table of acacia wood for the bread of the Presence (lehem ha-panim, bread of the face). Twelve loaves representing all Israel standing before YHWH's face continually. Jesus in John 6 identifies himself as the fulfillment: “I am the bread of life” (John 6:35, 48). The table of the Presence holds the bread that sustains Israel; Christ is the one who sustains his people through his own body (John 6:51-58; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26).
11: The table overlaid with pure gold with a gold molding around it. Gold covering the acacia table: divine dignity given to the place of provision. Every act of God's provision for his people is a divine act: the manna, the bread of the Presence, and Christ the Bread of Life are all YHWH's provision. The gold table is the type of the divine character of the provision Christ makes.
12: A rim a handbreadth wide around the table, and a gold molding around the rim. The boundary frame holding the bread in place. The covenant context within which Christ gives himself: he gives himself specifically to those who come to him (John 6:35: “whoever comes to me will never go hungry”). The Supper is bounded by the covenant community (1 Corinthians 11:23-29).
13: Rings of gold at the four corners where the four legs are. The table, like the ark, is equipped for transport: the presence of provision goes with the people. Christ's provision accompanies his people through the Lord's Supper: “For whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes” (1 Corinthians 11:26).
14: The rings close to the rim, holding the poles to carry the table. The bread of the Presence carried through the journey — always present. The ongoing availability of Christ as the Bread of Life throughout the whole journey of the church (Hebrews 13:8: “Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever”).
15: Poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold for carrying the table. The mechanism of Christ's presence brought to his people: the divine (gold) provision carried in the creaturely (acacia) vehicle. The gospel, carried by human messengers, brings the divine provision of Christ himself (2 Corinthians 4:7: “we have this treasure in jars of clay”).
16: The dishes, pans, bowls, and pitchers of pure gold — vessels for the drink offerings. All the vessels of service at the table are pure gold: the whole apparatus of provision is divine in character. The drink offering poured out: Christ poured out his life as a drink offering (Philippians 2:17; 2 Timothy 4:6). No human alloy in the provision.
17: The lampstand (menorah) of pure hammered gold, shaft and branches as one piece. Unlike the ark and table (acacia overlaid with gold), the lampstand is pure gold throughout — no underlying wood. Christ as the light of the world (John 8:12; Exodus 9:5) is not merely creaturely light elevated to divine use but the uncreated light of God himself (1 John 1:5: “God is light and in him there is no darkness at all”).
18: Six branches from the sides — three on one side and three on the other, giving seven lights total. The seven churches in Revelation 1:12-20 are seven lampstands; Christ walks among them as their light source. The church's witness is derivative of Christ's own: “You are the light of the world” (Matthew 5:14) — but only because he first said “I am the light of the world” (John 8:12).
19: Three cups made like almond blossoms on each branch. The almond-blossom design: the lampstand modeled on a flowering almond tree (cf. Numbers 17:8, Aaron's rod that blossomed). The almond (shaqed, the watcher/waker, blooming first in spring) symbolizes resurrection-life. Christ is the first to rise from the dead (1 Corinthians 15:20: “the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep”) — the flowering almond of the resurrection.
20: Four cups made like almond blossoms on the central shaft. The shaft bears four cups to the branches' three: the source bears more than its branches. Christ's own resurrection-life is greater than its reflection in the church: he is “the firstborn from the dead” (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5) from whom the resurrection of all who are his flows.
21: Under each pair of branches a calyx, one piece with it, for all six branches. The unity of the lampstand: branches and shaft are one piece. The church's unity with Christ is not external attachment but organic union: “I am the vine; you are the branches” (John 15:5). The branches of the lampstand are one with the shaft as the church is one with Christ.
22: Their calyxes and branches were of one piece with it — the whole a single piece of hammered pure gold. The lampstand as one unified object of pure gold: unity and purity together. Christ's prayer: “that they may be one as we are one — I in them and you in me — so that they may be brought to complete unity” (John 17:22-23).
23: Seven lamps, snuffers, and trays of pure gold. Maintenance tools also in gold: the whole economy of light-keeping is sacred. The letters to the seven churches in Rev 2-3 are Christ's lampstand-maintenance work — he tends the flames, trims the wicks, and warns churches whose light is in danger of going out (Revelation 2:5).
24: A talent of pure gold for the lampstand and all its utensils. The cost of the light is specified: one talent — approximately 34 kg of gold. The cost of the true light of the world is greater still: “you were redeemed... not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ” (1 Peter 1:18-19).
25: The altar of incense (mizbeach ha-kettoret) of acacia wood overlaid with pure gold. The incense altar stands before the veil burning fragrant incense continually before YHWH — the type of Christ's perpetual intercession: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25). The incense ascending = Christ's prayers for his people rising continually to the Father (Revelation 8:3-4).
26: Pure gold on the top, sides, and horns of the incense altar. The incense altar entirely gold: Christ's intercessory ministry is divine in character, not merely human pleading (Hebrews 7:26-27: “holy, innocent, undefiled, separated from sinners, exalted above the heavens”). The horns represent intercessory power extending in every direction (John 17:20: “I do not ask for these only, but also for those who will believe”).
27: Two gold rings and poles for carrying the incense altar. The incense altar is portable: Christ's intercession accompanies his people everywhere. He “is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us” (Romans 8:34) not from a geographically fixed location but from the universal locus of divine authority.
28: Poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold. The same construction: acacia (humanity) with gold (divinity). The incarnate Son's intercession: it is his human experience (“he knows our weakness, having been tempted in every way,” Hebrews 4:15) that makes his divine intercession effective. He pleads as one who has been where we are.
29: The holy anointing oil and the pure fragrant incense. The oil and incense: the anointing substance and the fragrance of worship. The holy anointing oil is the type of the Holy Spirit (1 John 2:20, 27: “you have an anointing from the Holy One”). Christ is the Anointed One (Mashiach): the Spirit descends on him at his baptism (Mark 1:10-11). The fragrant incense is the type of prayer ascending as a pleasing aroma (Psalms 141:2; Revelation 5:8).
